content.meaning: can - could - may - might - must - ought to - will - would - shall - should - have to - need - dare - used to

1. They take no -s in the third person singular except for have to, need and dare.
2. They have no infinitive or participle forms so they cannot be used in the continuous tenses except for "need", "have to" and "dare".
3. They are followed by bare infinitive except for ought to, have to and used to.
4. They form their negative and interrogative forms the same way as the auxiliaries (e.g. may not, may I ...?) apart from "need", "have to" and "dare".

+ e.g. I can see smoke in the distance.
+ e.g. She can't speak German.
+ e.g. When I was at school, I could play a lot. (repeated action)
+ e.g. He was able to escape through a window. (single action)
+ e.g. She wasn't able to/couldn't eat the sandwich. (Both types can be used in the negative for either a repeated or a single action.)

+ e.g. You can have a piece of cake if you want one.
+ e.g. We could ask someone for help.
+ e.g. He may be lucky this time.
+ e.g. She might come with us. (But I don't think so.)
+ e.g. You can't rely on British weather.
+ e.g. Is she likely to come?
+ e.g. Is it likely that she'll come?

+ e.g. Can I ask you a question?
+ e.g. Could you give me some advice?
+ e.g. May I make a suggestion?
+ e.g. Might I borrow your newspaper? (formal)
+ e.g. You can sit here if you'd like to.
+ e.g. You may take the last sweet.

+ e.g. She must pay the rent by Friday. (strong obligation or duty)
+ e.g. I have to speak to the boss. (obligation / necessity)
+ e.g. He had to have an X-ray.

+ e.g. I need to improve my French.
+ e.g. The house needs cleaning.
+ e.g. He has to make up his mind soon.
+ e.g. Must I go with you?
+ e.g. Do I have to finish this now?
+ e.g. Need he sign the form?

+ e.g. You mustn't eat in this room.
+ e.g. You are not to eat in this room. (= it's against the rules)
+ e.g. You can't join the tennis club. (= you aren't allowed to join the tennis club)

content.word: Functions: Absence of Obligation or Necessity
content.meaning:
+ e.g. You needn't worry - everything's under control.
+ e.g. I don't have to leave until 3 p.m.
+ e.g. You needn't have waited for me. (But you waited)
+ e.g. They didn't need to make any more food. (And they didn't)

+ e.g. He must be nervous about the test.
+ e.g. You must be feeling very sad.
+ e.g. She must have left by now.
+ e.g. He must have been lying all along.

+ e.g. It can't be yours - I'm sure it isn't.
+ e.g. She can't be enjoying herself; she looks so bored.
+ e.g. He can't have broken the window.
+ e.g. You can't have been listening properly.

+ e.g. You should take a holiday.
+ e.g. You ought to try harder.
+ e.g. You ought not to complain so much.

+ e.g. We should have made sure that the tickets were valid. (but we didn't)
+ e.g. He ought to have cooked dinner. (but he didn't)

+ e.g. Can you hurry up, please?
+ e.g. Would you pass me the salt?
+ e.g. I'll give you a lift.
+ e.g. Shall I make the tea?
+ e.g. She should go to the dentist's.
+ e.g. I might as well watch T.V.

content.word: Notes: Need, Dare, and Missing Tenses
content.meaning:
1. Need and dare are followed by a bare infinitive if they are used as modals, otherwise they are followed by a full infinitive.
+ e.g. You needn't come. or You don't need to come.
2. Be able to, be allowed to and have to are used to fill in the missing tenses of can, may (when expressing permission) and must respectively.

content.word: Notes: Was able to vs Could
content.meaning:
3. Was able to is used to refer to a past single action whereas could is used to refer to a past repeated action.
+ e.g. Although he was tired, he was able to swim to safety.
+ e.g. When he was young, he could swim well.

4. Have to expresses external obligation whereas must expresses obligation imposed by the speaker.
+ e.g. According to the law, all motorcyclists have to wear a helmet when riding their motorcycles.
+ e.g. I must wear my helmet when I ride my motorcycle or I'll be fined. (It's my decision to take the risk.)

5. may expresses possibility whereas might increases the doubt.
+ e.g. He may come if you ask him to.
+ e.g. She might come if she has time. (slight possibility)
